title: אִיתְגַּבַּר | Itgabar — a targum-piyyuṭ for introducing the sixth of the ten commandments on Shavuot
author: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also translates and authors his own original works.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (translation)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also authors his own original works and transcribes Hebrew and Aramaic text, adding niqqud and t'amim as needed.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Unknown
coauthors.bio: Sometimes the best we can do in attributing a historical work is to indicate the period and place it was written, the first prayer book it may have been printed in, or the archival collection in which the manuscript was found. We invite the public to help to attribute all works to their original composers. If you know something not mentioned in the commentary offered, please leave a comment or contact us.

excerpt: This piyyut, "Itgabar," was originally written as an introduction to the sixth commandment, the commandment of not murdering. It is a poetic retelling of the death of Joab in I Kings 2:28-34. Joab, the leader of David's armies, was a notoriously violent man, the slaughterer of Abner and Amasa. He died in a correspondingly violent way, struck down by Benaiah son of Jehoiada within the Tabernacle itself. Surprisingly enough, this poem frames Solomon as an unjust and cruel ruler, treating Joab's death as a tragedy rather than the quid-pro-quo consequence it is portrayed as in Kings. In this location, it almost goes so far as to calling Solomon himself a murderer.
content:
Nowadays, the only Jewish community with a custom to recite the Aramiac targum translation of the Torah portion is the Yemenites. But this was not always the case. For a very long time, Ashkenazim, Italkim, and some other related communities preserved a custom to read the targum on two specific days — the seventh day of Pesaḥ and the first day of Shavuot (Maḥzor Vitry, Hilkhot Pesaḥ 106:7). On those two days, mass communal miracles happened to the Jewish people — the splitting of the sea on the former, the revelation at Sinai on the latter — and the targum, the translation designed for the purpose of making the story accessible, was preserved for the sake of pirsumei nissa. Since the use of targum became a special rare occasion, Jews did what we do for special rare occasions — we write piyyutim. An extensive series of Aramaic piyyutim were written, to be inserted into the recitation of the targum (or the ‘seder meturgeman’) itself. On Shavuot, a massive series of piyyutim, one for every one of the ten commandments and then some, were written. On Pesaḥ, a similar number of piyyutim were composed for important moments. Since the mass acceptance of the Shulḥan Arukh, the custom of the recitation of the targum on these special days has been lost from Ashkenazi practice, and as a consequence the vast majority of the seder meturgeman piyyutim themselves have been abandoned. A small number of Shavuot seder meturgeman piyyutim are still in use (specifically, Aḳdamut Milin and Yetsiv Pitgam), but taken out of their original context. (As an example, why do we read Yetsiv Pitgam after the first verse of the haftarah? Because it’s meant to introduce the targum!) I personally love Aramaic, piyyutim, and liturgy, so I’ve taken it upon myself to translate a selection of seder meturgeman piyyutim.

This piyyut, “Itgabar,” was originally written as an introduction to the sixth commandment, the commandment of not murdering. It is a poetic retelling of the death of Joab in I Kings 2:28-34. Joab, the leader of David’s armies, was a notoriously violent man, the slaughterer of Abner and Amasa. He died in a correspondingly violent way, struck down by Benaiah son of Jehoiada within the Tabernacle itself. Surprisingly enough, this poem frames Solomon as an unjust and cruel ruler, treating Joab’s death as a tragedy rather than the quid-pro-quo consequence it is portrayed as in Kings. In this location, it almost goes so far as to calling Solomon himself a murderer.

The text here is as found in the Goldschmidt maḥzor, with some major textual variants (those that affect the meaning rather than just the spelling) marked. Also included is an phonetic alphabetical acrostic translation.

Source (Aramaic) Translation (English)
אִיתְגַּבַּר בְּחֵילֵיהּ אֲדוֹנִיָּה בַר־חַגִּית
כַּד־קָם בַּעֲצָתֵיהּ יוֹאָב בַּר־צְרוּיָה׃
Adonijah son of Ḥaggit strengthened in power,
when Joab son of Ẓeruiah came in his cunning.
בֵּינֵי טוּרַיָּא צְוַח יוֹאָב
וְכׇל־בִּרְיָתָא שָׁמְעִין קָלֵיהּ׃
Between the mountains shouted Joab,
and all creatures heard his voice.
גִּיבָּר הֲוֵינָא וְלֵית דִּכְוָתִי
וְכׇל־גְּבוּרְתִי מָה אַהְנֵית לִי׃
“Great am I, none is like me,
but what benefit is all my greatness to me?
דְּיָתֵיב מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה וְחָשֵׁיב עֲלַי לְמִקְטְלִי
וְלָא־צָבֵי שְׁמַע לְקַל מִילָּתִי׃
Doesn’t King Solomon sit on the throne, thinking to kill me,
unwilling to hear my voice’s words?
הֲלָא מִדְכַּר לִי מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה
כׇּל־טֵיבוּ דַּעֲבָדִית עִם־דָּוִד אֲבוּהִי׃
Ere all the good I did with David his father,
did King Solomon recall it on my behalf?
וְכַרְכִין וּמָחוֹזִין כַּבֵּישִׁית קֳדָמוֹהִי
נַחְלֵי יַמָּא וְכׇל־יַבֶּשְׁתָּא׃
When forts and ports I conquered before him,
rivers, ocean, and all dry land?
זָעוּ מִינִּי כׇּל־רַבְרְבַיָּא
וְכׇל־גִּיבָּרַיָּא עֲרַקוּ מִן־קֳדָמַי׃
Zipped away from me all the great ones,
and all the warrior fled from before me!
חֲבַל עַל־הָדֵין עָלְמָא אִם דֵּין הוּא דִינָא
דְּגִיבָּר דִּכְוָתִי מִתְקְטֵיל בְּחַרְבָּא׃
How shameful upon this world, if this is the rule,
that a warrior like me be killed by the sword!”
טְעַם יוֹאָב טַעְמָא דְּמוֹתָא
כַּד שְׁמַע אָמְרִין אֵיתוּ וְנִקְטְלִינֵּיהּ׃
Tasting the taste[1] Some texts say כַּסָא cup  of death, Joab,[2] In the Paris National Library 606 manuscript, everything from here to the third-to-last-line is replaced with an alternate shorter text: “עֲנֵה וַאֲמַר לְיוֹאֵל בְּרֵיה לְאִימָּךְ תְּיַקַר וְלָא־כַּעֲבַדְתְּ בְּחַיָּי וְאָחוּךְ תִּיזוּן וְלָא־תִּשְׁבּוֹק יָתְהוֹן מַה עֲבַד לִי מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה בְּדִינִין אַכְזָרַאִין דָּן יָתִי נְהַם יוֹאָב בְּהַהִיא שַׁעְתָּא וְכׇל־גִּיבָּרִין אָחְדוּ זֵינֵיהוֹן סְבוֹל גַּרְמָךְ וְלָא תִּקְטוֹל יָתִי עֲתִיד לְמִמְטֵיךְ זְמַן דְּתִדְכַּר יָתִי עִיוְתֵיהּ דְּאַבְנֵר בֶּן־נֵר לָא־תִדְכַּר לִי וְדַעֲמָשָׂא בֶּן־יֶתֶר לָא־תִּשְׁבַּק רוּחִי פְּתַח פּוּמֵיהּ יוֹאָב וַאֲמַר לִקְטוֹלֵיהּ בְּנָיָה בְּנָיָה הֵיךְ תִּקְטוֹל יָתִי צַוָּארִי תְּקֵיף וְסַיְפָךְ חֲלַשׁ וְהֵיךְ אַתְּ יָכִיל לִי לְמִקְטְלִי קִיצֵּץ בְּנָיָה רֵישֵׁיהּ דְּיוֹאָב וְאַעִיל יָתֵיה לְבֵי מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה רַעֲדוּ וְאִיתְבְּהִילוּ כׇּל־גִּיבָּרִין וּבְכוֹ כׇּל־רַבְרְבָנֵי דְּמַלְכָּא שָׁמְעוּ…” (…he called for and said to Joel his son: “Let your mother be honored, not like you did in my life, and care for your brothers, do not abandon them. My, what did King Solomon do to me? With cruel laws he judged me!”Now Joab roared at that time, and all the warriors grabbed their weapons, “Suffer my existence, do not kill me! To you time will come when you remember me! Of Abner son of Ner’s error do not recall against me, nor take my life for Amasa son of Jether!” Prying open his mouth, Joab said to his killer, “Benaiah! Benaiah! How will you kill me? Chunky is my neck, your sword is weak, and how can you chance to kill me? Quickly Benaiah cut off Joab;’s head, and brought it to the house of King Solomon. Rattled, all the warriors were shaken, and all the king’s great ones wept. Shouting…) 
when he heard them saying “Come on, let’s kill him!”
יָאֵי דִינְכוֹן דְּתִקְטְלוּן יָתִי
בָּתַר דִּקְטֵילִית כׇּל־שַׂנְאֵיכוֹן׃
“Nice.[3] Sarcasm.  Your rule is to kill me,
after I killed all your enemies!”
כַּד חֲמָא יוֹאָב דִּמְקַטְּלִין יָתֵיהּ
שָׁרִי צָוַח לְיוֹאֵל בְּרֵיהּ׃
Consequently when Joab saw they would kill him,
he started shouting for Joel[4] A name found nowhere earlier to this poem for the son of Joab.  his son.
לָךְ יוֹאֵל בְּרִי אֲנָא מְפַקֵּד יָתָךְ
אוֹרָיְתָא תִיטַּר וְלַאֲחוּךְ תְּרַבֵּי וּלְאִמָּךְ תְּיַקַּר׃
“Look, Joel, my son, I’m ordering you:
keep the Torah, raise your brothers, honor your mother.
מַה אַעֲבֵיד לֵיהּ לְמַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה
אֵיךְ הוּא אַכְזָר לְמִיקְטַל יָתִי׃
Me, what can I do against King Solomon?
How is he so cruel as to kill me?”
נְהַם יוֹאָב בְּשַׁעְתָּא הַהִיא
וְאִישְׁתְּמַע קָלֵיהּ בְּכׇל־אַרְעָא דְּיִשְׂרָאֵל׃
Now at that time Joab roared,
and his voice was heard in all the land of Israel.[5] Some texts say כַּד חֲמָא גִּיבָּרַיָּא דְּאָחְדִין זֵינֵיהוֹן when the warriors saw that they were holding their weapons 
סְבוֹל גַּרְמָךְ וְלָא תִקְטְלִינַּנִי
דַּעֲתִיד לְמֵיתֵי יוֹמָא דְּתִידְכַּר יָתִי׃
“Suffer yourself not to kill me,
for a day will come when you will remember me.”
עֲנִי לֵיה מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה
דְּכִיר לָךְ דְּמָא דְאַבְנֵר בֶּן־נֵר
וְדַעֲמָשָׂא בֶּן־יֶתֶר לָא־תַּשְׁנִיק רוּחָךְ׃
Opined King Solomon,
“Remember Abner son of Ner’s blood?
Does Amasa son of Jether not cross your mind?”
פְּתַח פּוּמֵיהּ יוֹאָב בַּר־צְרוּיָה
וַאֲזַל וּבְכָא עִם־כׇּל־חַבְרוֹהִי׃
Prying open his mouth, Joab son of Zeruiah[6] Some texts say בְּשַׁעְתֵּיהּ הַהִיא at his time 
went and cried with all his friends.
צְוַח יוֹאָב בְּשַׁעְתָּא הַהִיא
וְכׇל־גִּיבָּרַיָּא נְטַלוּ זֵינֵיהוֹן׃
Screamed Joab at that time,
and all the warriors took up their arms.[7] Some texts say וְאִישְׁתְּמַע קָלֵיהּ בְּכׇל־אַרְעָא and his voice was heard in all the land 
קָלֵיהּ אֲרֵים מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה
וְשָׁרִי צָוַח לִרְחַבְעָם בְּרֵיהּ׃
Quickened was King Solomon by his voice,
and he started shouting for Rehoboam his son.
רְחַבְעָם בְּרִי קוּם שְׁלוֹף זֵינָךְ
וְתִידְכַּר לָךְ דְּמָא דְאַבְשָׁלוֹם דּוֹדָךְ׃
“Rehoboam my son! Get up, draw your weapon,
and remember your uncle Absalom’s blood!”
שָׁמְעִין קָלֵיהּ כׇּל־יִשְׂרָאֵל
שָׁרְיָן עֵינֵיהוֹן דִּמְעִין מְדַלְּגִין׃
Shed tears began to flow from their eyes,
as all Israel heard his voice.
תְּמַהּ שְׁלֹמֹה וַאֲמַר בְּלִיבֵּיהּ
מַה אִיעֲבֵיד לִשְׁבוּעֲתָא דְּאַבָּא׃
Thus Solomon was shocked and said in his heart,
“What is to be done of my father’s oath?”[8] Older texts conclude here in medias res. The remaining lines, found in later texts, conclude the story. 
כַּד שְׁמַע יוֹאָב בַּר־צְרוּיָה
פְּתַח פּוּמֵיהּ יוֹאָב שָׁרִי צָוַח לִבְנָיָהוּ בֶן־יְהוֹיָדָע
When Joab son of Zeruiah heard,
prying open his mouth Joab started to shout for Benaiah son of Jehoiada.
בְּנָיָה בְּנָיָה לָא־תַשְׁנִיק רוּחָךְ
צַוָּארִי תְּקֵיף וְסַיְפָךְ חֲלִישׁ וְלָא אַתְּ יָכֵיל לְמִיקְטַל יָתִי׃
“Benaiah! Benaiah! Don’t let it cross your mind!
Chunky is my neck, your sword is weak, and you cannot kill me!”
קִיצֵּץ בְּנָיָה רֵישֵׁיהּ דְּיוֹאָב
וְרֵישֵיהּ דְּיוֹאָב עָל לְבֵית מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה׃
Quickly, Benaiah sliced off Joab’s head,
and Joab’s head rushed to King Solomon’s house.
שְׁמָעוּ עַמְמַיָּא דְּאִיקְּטֵיל יוֹאָב
בְּפוּמְהוֹן חַדְיָן וּבְלִיבְּהוֹן בַּכְיָן׃
Shouting with joy were the peoples[9] The nations, who were his enemy, but respected his courage. 
when they heard Joab was dead, but their hearts wept.
תְּוַהוֹ וּבְכוֹ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל בִּכְיָא רַבָּא
עַל־דְּאִיתְקְטֵיל יוֹאָב בַּר־צְרוּיָה׃
Thus Israel shook and wept great tears,
that Joab son of Zeruiah was killed.

 

Notes

Notes
1 Some texts say כַּסָא cup
2 In the Paris National Library 606 manuscript, everything from here to the third-to-last-line is replaced with an alternate shorter text: “עֲנֵה וַאֲמַר לְיוֹאֵל בְּרֵיה לְאִימָּךְ תְּיַקַר וְלָא־כַּעֲבַדְתְּ בְּחַיָּי וְאָחוּךְ תִּיזוּן וְלָא־תִּשְׁבּוֹק יָתְהוֹן מַה עֲבַד לִי מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה בְּדִינִין אַכְזָרַאִין דָּן יָתִי נְהַם יוֹאָב בְּהַהִיא שַׁעְתָּא וְכׇל־גִּיבָּרִין אָחְדוּ זֵינֵיהוֹן סְבוֹל גַּרְמָךְ וְלָא תִּקְטוֹל יָתִי עֲתִיד לְמִמְטֵיךְ זְמַן דְּתִדְכַּר יָתִי עִיוְתֵיהּ דְּאַבְנֵר בֶּן־נֵר לָא־תִדְכַּר לִי וְדַעֲמָשָׂא בֶּן־יֶתֶר לָא־תִּשְׁבַּק רוּחִי פְּתַח פּוּמֵיהּ יוֹאָב וַאֲמַר לִקְטוֹלֵיהּ בְּנָיָה בְּנָיָה הֵיךְ תִּקְטוֹל יָתִי צַוָּארִי תְּקֵיף וְסַיְפָךְ חֲלַשׁ וְהֵיךְ אַתְּ יָכִיל לִי לְמִקְטְלִי קִיצֵּץ בְּנָיָה רֵישֵׁיהּ דְּיוֹאָב וְאַעִיל יָתֵיה לְבֵי מַלְכָּא שְׁלֹמֹה רַעֲדוּ וְאִיתְבְּהִילוּ כׇּל־גִּיבָּרִין וּבְכוֹ כׇּל־רַבְרְבָנֵי דְּמַלְכָּא שָׁמְעוּ…” (…he called for and said to Joel his son: “Let your mother be honored, not like you did in my life, and care for your brothers, do not abandon them. My, what did King Solomon do to me? With cruel laws he judged me!”Now Joab roared at that time, and all the warriors grabbed their weapons, “Suffer my existence, do not kill me! To you time will come when you remember me! Of Abner son of Ner’s error do not recall against me, nor take my life for Amasa son of Jether!” Prying open his mouth, Joab said to his killer, “Benaiah! Benaiah! How will you kill me? Chunky is my neck, your sword is weak, and how can you chance to kill me? Quickly Benaiah cut off Joab;’s head, and brought it to the house of King Solomon. Rattled, all the warriors were shaken, and all the king’s great ones wept. Shouting…)
3 Sarcasm.
4 A name found nowhere earlier to this poem for the son of Joab.
5 Some texts say כַּד חֲמָא גִּיבָּרַיָּא דְּאָחְדִין זֵינֵיהוֹן when the warriors saw that they were holding their weapons
6 Some texts say בְּשַׁעְתֵּיהּ הַהִיא at his time
7 Some texts say וְאִישְׁתְּמַע קָלֵיהּ בְּכׇל־אַרְעָא and his voice was heard in all the land
8 Older texts conclude here in medias res. The remaining lines, found in later texts, conclude the story.
9 The nations, who were his enemy, but respected his courage.
